english: ass; boṅ-sgál (bong sgal) an ass's load; boṅ-sbáṅ (bong sbang) dung of an ass; boṅ-rdzí (bong rdzi) keeper or driver of an ass; dre-bóṅ (dre bong) Cs. 'an ass generating a mule'. — 2. n. of insects, rgyás-pai bóṅ-bu (rgyas pai bong bu) sugar-mite, lepisma, Ld.; boṅ-nág (bong nag) (perh. buṅ-nág (bung nag)) dung-beetle Lh. — 3. Cs.: blockhead, fool.

1. Ssk. भोट, Tibet, bód-(kyi) yul (bod (kyi) yul) id. 2. for bód-pa (bod pa), bod k̇a-ċig (bod kha cig) some Tibetans Tar., k̇yed bód-rnams (khyed bod rnams) ye Tibetans. — 3. for bód-skad (bod skad) the Tibetan language, bód-du bsgyur jug (bod du bsgyur 'jug) I will have it translated into Tibetan Pth.; bód-skad (bod skad), in a more limited sense, also implies the common language of conversation, opp. to book-language W.; bód-pa (bod pa), bód-(kyi) mi (bod (kyi) mi) Tibetans, bód-mo (bod mo) fem; bód-kyi mi-rigs (bod kyi mi rigs) or mi-brgyúd (mi brgyud) the people of Tibet, in contradistinction to other nations, bód-_obaṅs (bod 'bangs) the Tibetan people, opp. to its ruler.
1. v. bod (bod). — 2. = _obód-pa ('bod pa).

(acc. to Schf. = बीज) 1. n. of the early religion of Tibet, concerning which but very imperfect accounts are existing (v. Report of the Royal Bavarian Acad. of Sc., 13. Jan. 1866); so much is certain, that sorcery was the principal feature of it. When Buddhism became the religion of state, the former was considered heretical and condemnable, and lha-čos (lha chos) and bon-čos (bon chos), or shorter čos (chos) and bon (bon), were placed in opposition, as with us christianity and paganism; v. Glr. and Mil.; at the present time, both of them seem to exist peaceably side by side, and the primitive religion has not only numerous adherents and convents in C., but manifold traces of it may be found still in the creed of the Tibetans of to-day. — 2. = bón-po (bon po), follower of this religion.
v. _obór-ba ('bor ba).
a sack of corn, holding about 30 k̇al (khal) W.
bol-góṅ (bol gong) 1. the upper part of the foot Stg. — 2. the leg of a boot W. — 3. clod of earth C. — 4. v. _obol ('bol).
= búl-ha-ri (bul ha ri).
v. _oból-po ('bol po).
v. _obód-pa ('bod pa).

1. sbst. bird, fowl, hen, cf. the following articles. (Pur. biu (biu) [v. byiu (byiu)], bi (bi)). — 2. vb. fut. root of byéd-pa (byed pa), v. this and the sbst. byá-ba (bya ba). — 3. *ja ċó-ċe* (ja co ce) W., to castrate, to geld.
prop.: explanation, 1. = luṅ-du-ston-pa (lung du ston pa) prophecy, cf. Burn. I, 54 sequ. — 2. in later times: grammar.
Mṅ., bya-tri (bya tri) Lt., n. of a medicine.
1. a bird's foot. — 2. n. of a vein Med. — 3. officinal plant, in Lh. a blue kind of orobanche.
also bya-sgrúṅs (bya sgrungs), bya-čós (bya chos) title of a book of satirical fables, in which birds are introduced speaking.
fowler's net Lex.
Stg.; Sch.: roof, shelter.
Cs. bird-cage.
= k̇yuṅ (khyung), k̇ra (khra) and k̇ruṅ-k̇rúṅ (khrung khrung).
Dzl. and elsewh., a species of ducks, Sch.: the gray duck.
gift, present, esp. as a reward; sbyín-pa (sbyin pa) to bestow a gift, frq.; bya-dgár (bya dgar) as a present, for a reward, stér-ba (ster ba) to give.

bird of prey B. and col.; bya-rgod-spos (bya rgod spos) Med., vulgo la-da-ra (la da ra) (v. gla (gla)) bya-rgod-ṗúṅ-poi ri (bya rgod phung poi ri), गृध्रकूट, vulture-hill, in Magadha, a preaching-place of Buddha.
fowler's net.
n. of one of the smaller lobes of the lungs.
v. bya-rmyéṅ (bya rmyeng).
v. bya-krí (bya kri).
Glr. light-gray bird's dung.
